name: Apris
classification_notes: Schick Toikka frame Apris as a contemporary take on the 19th-century Latines genre, marked by pointed serifs and capitals of harmonised widths, with concave serifs, acute trumpet-bell or thorn-like terminals and shoulders that taper to a point.
summary: A contemporary Latin serif with concave, pointed serifs and acute trumpet-bell terminals, drawn in six weights from Thin to Black with matching italics.
description: Apris is a present-day reading of the 19th-century Latines genre (also known as Renaissance- or Etienne-Schriften, Latin and Runic, or Celtic). Concave serifs, shoulders that taper to a point and long-drawn unseriffed terminals give it a sharp, almost prickly silhouette. The italic follows an inclined-roman model with no cursive or narrowed shapes, in line with the historical precedent that Latines rarely had a true italic. The family spans six weights from Thin to Black with matching italics: lighter cuts can signal luxury and loftiness, the medium cuts read as self-evident shop-sign and nameplate type, and the Bold and Black get funkier with overtones of 1970s display typography. Stylistic Sets offer monocular alternates for a and g and an R with a pointed leg.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Apris as a contemporary typeface that adopts features of the 1850s Latines genre, with moderate stroke contrast on a vertical axis and concave serifs whose stems and arms flare into acute ends resembling trumpet bells or thorns. They cite German Mediaeval and Antiqua details from around 1910, frame the original Latines as jobbing and titling type for cards and letterheads, and note that Apris extends the genre to print and screen across six weights with matching italics.

name: Chap
classification_notes: Schick Toikka position Chap as a tribute to the contrasted sans tradition of the 1920s to 1950s, originally drawn for the Finnish Culture Institute in New York; thicks and thins generally reference the broad-nib pen but skirt convention with unusual twists of stroke and moments where calligraphy yields to geometry.
summary: A modern contrast sans, originally drawn for the Finnish Culture Institute in New York, in six weights with italics and figures for text, titling and tables.
description: Chap is a modulated grotesk that pays tribute to the visibly contrasted sans serifs popular in the 1920s to 1950s, when they were widely used in advertising, architectural lettering and logotypes. It avoids direct revival, with thicks and thins that generally reference the broad-nib pen but break convention with unusual twists of stroke and stretches where calligraphy yields to geometry. Aimed at multipurpose work rather than display fashion, Chap reads cleanly in passages of text at a range of sizes, supported by six weights with italics, figure sets for text, titling and tables, and an Extended Latin character set.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Chap as initially designed for the Finnish Culture Institute in New York, paying tribute to the contrasted sans tradition without referencing any single design. The story they tell is one of stroke contrast in 20th-century grotesks gradually disappearing under late Modernism and only resurfacing in service of novelty or nostalgia, until Chap reasserts a modern, current contrasted sans built for readable text in all sizes and lengths.

name: Dia
classification_notes: Schick Toikka frame Dia as a family that captures the spirit of mid-1800s jobbing grotesks, when printers mixed and matched sans serifs of varying shapes and sizes; the four weights deliberately do not adhere to a uniform pattern, each celebrating a different historical model.
summary: A four-weight early-grotesk revival that mixes distinct historical models in one family, with hairline accents, circled numbers and arrows.
description: Dia distils the diverse spirit of the first jobbing grotesks of the mid-1800s into a concise eight-style family that works for both text and display. The four weights with matching italics deliberately avoid a uniform pattern: each celebrates a different historical model, from light and narrow with curling terminals to heavy and broad with ends that point outward. Compatible vertical proportions let them sit harmoniously in the same line, and an alternate set of hairline accents and punctuation can dial up the expressive register. The OpenType feature set includes figures and fractions, an Extended Latin character set and a batch of circled numbers and arrows.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Dia as warm and mildly quirky, an antidote to the cold, calculated air of contemporary type that nonetheless does not sacrifice contemporary conveniences. They cite the hodgepodge fashion of mid-1800s printing as the family's reference point, and note that Dia's eight fonts share warmth and personality even though each weight references a different historical Grot.

name: Edict
classification_notes: Schick Toikka describe Edict as an original interpretation of late Renaissance and early Baroque French type rather than an academic revival; the principal prototype cited is Jean Jannon, whose work was the actual basis for several of the 20th-century Garamonds. Edict shows higher contrast and a contrast axis that is not evenly inclined, characteristics of so-called Transitional type.
summary: A high-contrast French Garalde with sharp serifs, swelling teardrop terminals and full swash italic, in Display and Text optical sizes plus a variable font.
description: Edict is an original interpretation of late Renaissance and early Baroque French type rather than a revival of any single source. Its closest prototype is Jean Jannon, whose work in turn underpinned several of the 20th-century Garamonds, and Edict shows the higher contrast and unevenly inclined axis that mark Transitional type. At its heart is the syncopation of sharp serifs and swelling teardrop terminals, with all outer corners ever so slightly rounded for a radiant warmth. The italic is animated but optimised for tight present-day fit and offers full swash capitals and lowercase terminal forms; small caps are included for advanced typesetting. Edict is available in Display and Text styles, each with five static weights (Text adds an extra Book weight), and a variable font with weight and optical size axes.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Edict as bringing chiaroscuro overtones to a contemporary serif: bold weights did not exist in the 16th and 17th centuries, but Edict Bold's increased contrast brings to mind the light-dark technique popular with painters of the era. The foundry frame the Display and Text optical sizes as a working system, with the Text style including an additional Book weight and a variable font supporting weight and optical size axes.

name: Edict Display
classification_notes: The Display optical size of Edict, with the family's higher contrast and sharper detail tuned for larger sizes; based on Jean Jannon and the wider French Garalde-Transitional tradition, with sharp serifs and swelling teardrop terminals.
summary: Edict's display optical size: the sharper, higher-contrast cut for headlines, set in five weights with full swash italic.
description: Edict Display is the larger-size cut of the Edict family, drawing on Jean Jannon and wider French Garalde-Transitional sources to deliver sharper serifs, more pronounced teardrop terminals and finer hairlines for headlines and titling. Five static weights with matching italics include opulent swash capitals and lowercase terminal forms, plus small caps for advanced typesetting. Edict Display sits at the top of a hierarchy that extends down through Edict Text for body sizes; both subfamilies share Latin Extended language support and OpenType figure sets.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Edict as an original interpretation of late Renaissance and early Baroque French type rather than an academic revival, citing Jean Jannon as the principal prototype. They frame the Display style as part of a system across Display and Text optical sizes, each with five static weights and matching italics, and flag the family's signature interplay of sharp serifs and swelling teardrop terminals.

name: Edict Text
classification_notes: The Text optical size of Edict, with sturdier proportions and lower contrast for body copy; built on Jean Jannon and the wider French Garalde-Transitional tradition. Includes an additional Book weight that the Display style does not.
summary: Edict's text optical size: a sturdier Garalde-Transitional companion for body copy, with an extra Book weight on top of the Display range.
description: Edict Text is the body-size optical companion to Edict Display, drawn on the same Jean Jannon and French Garalde-Transitional prototypes but with sturdier strokes, lower contrast and broader proportions for legibility at small sizes. The cut runs across six static weights including an additional Book weight that the Display style does not carry, with matching italics, swash capitals, lowercase terminal forms and small caps. Latin Extended language support and a full OpenType figure set make it usable in long-form editorial settings, paired up to Edict Display for headlines.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka note that Edict Text adds an additional Book weight to the family's six-weight range, and frame the Display and Text optical sizes as a system for building hierarchies from titling down to body. They emphasise that Edict is an original interpretation of French Renaissance and Baroque type, with Jannon as principal prototype, rather than a revival of any single source.

name: Items
classification_notes: Schick Toikka tie Items to the pervasive serifs of the early digital period, with Times New Roman as the central reference: Times shipped on the first laser printers from IBM and Xerox and on Apple's LaserWriter, was a core font in Microsoft Windows from 1990, and was for many years one of just two feasible options for serif text on the web.
summary: A Times-lineage transitional serif system in Display and Text branches, three widths each, plus monospace styles and pixel-icon extras evoking 1980s DTP.
description: Items is a contemporary serif tied to the pervasive serifs of the early digital period, drawn to overcome the limitations of Times and its peers. A consistent contrast axis and constant top-serif angle across weights, plus carefully modulated blacks and shapely whites, give it an even typographic colour. The family has two branches: Items, with tight letter fit for larger sizes and tapered joints, and Items Text, with subdued contrast and open spacing for body copy. Each branch offers three widths (Standard, Condensed, Wide) in seven weights with matching italics, plus a Monospace style with fixed proportions and spindly strokes that nods to Latin glyphs in fonts made for Chinese and Japanese scripts. A variable version covers optical size and weight axes. Extras include ligatures, alternates, multiple numeral sets and a pixel set of arrows, punctuation and icons that reads as a homage to Susan Kare.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Items as reconciling familiarity with beauty: it ties in with the early DTP era when Garamond, Goudy and Times shaped print ads, logos and startup screens, and pushes the genre forward with consistent contrast, multiple widths and a Monospace style. They flag the Condensed Black as recalling the Times variant on Fleckhaus paperback designs and the spindly Mono as a reference to the Latin glyphs in CJK fonts.

name: Items Condensed
classification_notes: The Condensed width of Items, the display branch of the Items system; tied to early-digital serifs (Times, Garamond, Goudy) and built on a consistent contrast axis with constant top-serif angle.
summary: The condensed display cut of the Items system: a tight transitional serif for headlines, with a Condensed Black that nods to the Times variant on Fleckhaus paperbacks.
description: Items Condensed is the condensed width of the Items display branch, with a tight letter fit and tapered joints aimed at larger sizes. Six weights from Thin to Black with matching italics give it the range to handle headlines, mastheads and packaging where horizontal economy matters. The Condensed Black in particular is described by the foundry as having qualities similar to the punchy Times variant seen on paperback designs by Fleckhaus. It sits inside a wider Items system that also extends into Standard and Wide widths and into the Items Text branch for body sizes.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka frame Items as reconciling familiarity with beauty by tying in with the pervasive serifs of the early digital period. They specifically describe the Condensed Black as recalling Helmut Salden and Willy Fleckhaus-era German paperback design, the punchy Times variant used at the time.

name: Items Standard
classification_notes: The Standard width of Items, the display branch of the Items system; tight letter fit and tapered joints, optimised for titles and headlines and tied to the early-digital serif tradition.
summary: The standard width of the Items display branch: a Times-lineage transitional serif for headlines, with tight letter fit and tapered joints across six weights and italics.
description: Items Standard is the standard-width display cut of the Items family. Tight letter fit and slick tapered joints optimise it for crisp titles and headlines, and six weights from Thin to Black with matching italics extend its range across editorial hierarchy. It connects to Items Condensed and Items Wide on the width axis and to the Items Text branch for body sizes, all sharing the same constant top-serif angle and consistent contrast axis that the foundry use to overcome the limitations of Times and its peers.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe the Items display branch (which Items Standard sits in) as optimised for larger applications, with slick details such as the tapered joints making for a crisp look in titles and headlines. They contrast it with Items Text, whose more robust build and subdued stroke contrast suit body sizes.

name: Items Text
classification_notes: The Text branch of Items, with subdued stroke contrast, open spacing and an additional Book weight; designed for pleasant performance in body sizes within the wider Times-lineage Items system.
summary: The text optical-size branch of Items: a sturdier transitional serif for body copy, with subdued contrast, open spacing and seven weights including Book.
description: Items Text is the text-size branch of the Items system. A more robust build, subdued stroke contrast and open spacing make it suitable for body copy and long-form reading. Seven weights from Thin to Black, including an extra Book weight, are matched with italics, and the cut sits inside a wider Items system that extends across Condensed, Standard and Wide widths in both Display and Text branches. Latin Extended language support, multiple numeral sets and a pixel set of arrows, punctuation and icons echoing Susan Kare's work round it out.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Items Text as distinguished by a more robust build, with subdued stroke contrast and open spacing that ensure pleasant performance in text sizes. They frame the Items Text branch as the body-copy partner to the tighter, larger-size Items display branch.

name: Items Text Condensed
classification_notes: The condensed width of the Items Text branch: subdued contrast and open spacing of Items Text, narrowed for body copy where horizontal economy matters.
summary: The condensed text cut of Items Text: a narrowed transitional serif for tight body copy across seven weights with italics.
description: Items Text Condensed combines the body-copy character of Items Text with a condensed width. Subdued contrast, open spacing relative to its width and a sturdier build keep it readable at small sizes, while seven weights including Book give it editorial range. It sits inside the wider Items system across Standard, Condensed and Wide widths and across Display and Text branches, with Latin Extended language support and a full OpenType feature set.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe the Items Text branch as the body-copy partner to the family's display widths, with the Condensed style narrowing the proportions for tight columns of running text. The wider Items system reconciles familiarity with beauty by tying in with the pervasive serifs of the early digital period, particularly Times.

name: Items Text Wide
classification_notes: The wide cut of the Items Text branch; subdued stroke contrast and open spacing of the text branch carried into a wider proportion, exploring what a transitional serif looks like when freed from space constraints.
summary: The wide text cut of Items Text: a roomier transitional serif for body copy, exploring what Times-lineage forms look like when freed from space constraints.
description: Items Text Wide widens the body-copy proportions of the Items Text branch. The foundry describe Items Wide as opening a whole new dimension, exploring what a transitional serif would look like if freed from space constraints; the Text version brings that wider stance into running text, with subdued contrast and open spacing for legibility. Seven weights including Book are matched with italics, and the cut connects to Items Standard and Items Condensed and to the display branch as part of the wider Items system.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Items Wide as exploring what a transitional serif would look like if freed from space constraints. The Items Text branch keeps the family's robust build, subdued contrast and open spacing, with Items Text Wide carrying those qualities into a wider width for spacious editorial settings.

name: Items Wide
classification_notes: The Wide width of the Items display branch; the foundry describe it as opening a whole new dimension, exploring what a transitional serif looks like if freed from space constraints.
summary: The wide cut of the Items display branch: a spacious transitional serif for headlines, exploring Times-lineage forms freed from space constraints.
description: Items Wide widens the proportions of the Items display branch. Six weights from Thin to Black with matching italics keep the family's tight letter fit and tapered joints intact while opening up the horizontal stance, which the foundry describe as exploring what a transitional serif looks like when freed from space constraints. Items Wide pairs naturally with Items Standard and Items Condensed in the display branch and with the Items Text branch for body sizes, supported by Latin Extended language coverage.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Items Wide as opening a whole new dimension, exploring what a transitional serif would look like if freed from space constraints. They place it within the Items system alongside the Standard and Condensed widths and a parallel Items Text branch for body copy.

name: Krana Fat
classification_notes: Schick Toikka describe Krana Fat as a peculiar display typeface inspired by the lettering of Finnish graphic designer and illustrator Erkki Toukolehto, sitting between typography and lettering and evoking vernacular signage, hobbyist templates and institutional alphabets constructed by teams of engineers.
summary: A two-style brush-derived display face with condensed (A) and extended (B) cuts, designed to be mixed; first deployed in Bremen's 'Die Zeitschrift der Strasse'.
description: Krana Fat is a display typeface inspired by the lettering of Finnish designer and illustrator Erkki Toukolehto. Rather than imitating his wide flat brush, it abstracts the strokes into a soft contour and lands somewhere between typography and lettering, evoking vernacular signage, hobbyist templates and engineered institutional alphabets. A prototype first ran in the award-winning 'Die Zeitschrift der Strasse' in Bremen, where condensed and wide caps were mixed for staccato headlines. The released family splits this into two fonts, A (condensed) and B (extended), each with a lowercase, designed to be mixed within a line. Two sets of compact alternate accents stack closely above the cap line for dense type or maximum-size letters per page.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Krana Fat as an homage to Toukolehto's lettering rather than an imitation: there are no faux bristles, no attempt to mimic handmade strokes. They flag the magazine origin in 'Die Zeitschrift der Strasse', the deliberate mixing of A and B styles within a sentence, and the compact alternate accents for stacking dense lines or filling pages with the largest possible letters.

name: Lateral
classification_notes: Schick Toikka describe Lateral as a contemporary superfamily revolving around sideways expansion, naming it after the Latin lateralis. They cite Adrian Frutiger's Univers and his cut-and-shift technique, and note that Lateral is the foundry's first variable font family, conceived for the format from the start; all curves have a flat section at their extremes which becomes more visible as widths expand.
summary: A 98-style neo-grotesque superfamily across seven widths and seven weights, with a variable font controlling width, weight and slant via 56 masters.
description: Lateral is Schick Toikka's first variable family, conceived for the format from the start. The widest width takes up more than five times the space of the narrowest, and all curves share a flat section at their extremes that becomes increasingly visible the further the width axis is pushed, yielding straight-sided rounds at the compressed end and wide horizontals at the expanded end. The systematic approach echoes Frutiger's Univers and his cut-and-shift technique, and gives every line a slightly boxy, brutalist edge. The variable font drives three axes (width, weight, slant) from 56 meticulously drawn masters; the static range is a 98-style workhorse spanning seven widths in seven weights with upright and italic variants. Middle widths handle all-purpose typesetting; the extreme widths are made for display.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka frame Lateral as a superfamily revolving around sideways expansion, with the name taken from the Latin lateralis (to the side). They cite Univers and Frutiger's cut-and-shift method, flag the planed rounds and horizontal exit strokes as adding brutalist overtones, and describe the variable font as driven by 56 meticulously drawn masters across width, weight and slant.

name: Lateral Compressed
classification_notes: The Compressed width within the seven-width Lateral system; flat curve segments yield straight-sided rounds at compressed widths.
summary: The Compressed width of Lateral: a tight neo-grotesque cut with straight-sided rounds, in seven weights with italics.
description: Lateral Compressed is one of the seven widths in the Lateral superfamily. At this point along the width axis the flat segments at curve extremes become clearly visible, giving the cut its straight-sided rounds and a brutalist edge. Seven weights from Thin to Black with matching italics suit display work where horizontal economy matters, and the cut connects to Ultra Compressed and Condensed on the width axis and to the Standard, Extended, Expanded and Ultra Wide widths through the variable font.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Lateral as a contemporary superfamily revolving around sideways expansion. They flag the planed rounds and horizontal exit strokes as adding brutalist overtones at every width, and frame the system as drawn from a single design principle adhered to across all widths and weights.

name: Lateral Condensed
classification_notes: The Condensed width within the seven-width Lateral system; sits between Compressed and Standard.
summary: The Condensed width of Lateral: a tight workhorse neo-grotesque between Compressed and Standard, in seven weights with italics.
description: Lateral Condensed sits one notch wider than Lateral Compressed and one notch narrower than Lateral Standard, within the seven-width Lateral system. The flat curve segments are still visible enough to read as a deliberate, slightly boxy detail, lending the cut a quietly brutalist edge while keeping it usable for editorial settings. Seven weights from Thin to Black with matching italics handle hierarchy from text to display, and the cut connects through the Lateral variable font to all other widths and to a slant axis.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Lateral as Schick Toikka's first variable font family, conceived for the format from the start. They flag the design's adherence to a single principle across all 98 static styles and 56 variable masters, and frame the middle widths as suitable for all-purpose typesetting.

name: Lateral Expanded
classification_notes: The Expanded width within the seven-width Lateral system; flat curve segments stretch into wide horizontals at expanded widths.
summary: The Expanded width of Lateral: a stretched neo-grotesque with wide horizontals, in seven weights with italics, sized for display use.
description: Lateral Expanded sits towards the wide end of the seven-width Lateral system. The flat segments at curve extremes are pulled into pronounced horizontal bars, giving the letterforms a slow, brutalist stance that the foundry mark out as display territory. Seven weights from Thin to Black with matching italics keep the cut versatile across hierarchy, and the wider Lateral variable font allows continuous interpolation between this width and Extended, Ultra Wide, and the rest of the system.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Lateral as a contemporary superfamily revolving around sideways expansion, with the wider widths producing letterforms with wide-stretching horizontal parts and the extreme widths lending themselves to display use.

name: Lateral Extended
classification_notes: The Extended width within the seven-width Lateral system; sits between Standard and Expanded.
summary: The Extended width of Lateral: a wider workhorse neo-grotesque between Standard and Expanded, in seven weights with italics.
description: Lateral Extended sits one notch wider than Lateral Standard and one notch narrower than Lateral Expanded, within the seven-width Lateral system. The flat segments at curve extremes are clearly visible at this width, giving the cut its boxy, brutalist undertone while remaining workable for editorial as well as display use. Seven weights from Thin to Black with matching italics give it the range to handle hierarchy at this width, with continuous interpolation through the Lateral variable font.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Lateral as Schick Toikka's first variable font family. They flag that the middle widths are intended for all-purpose typesetting while the extreme ones lean into display, with a single design principle adhered to across the family.

name: Lateral Standard
classification_notes: The central width within the seven-width Lateral system; the flat curve segments are short and of little visible consequence at this width.
summary: The central width of Lateral: a workhorse neo-grotesque in seven weights with italics, where the family's flat curve segments are subtlest.
description: Lateral Standard is the central width of the seven-width Lateral system. At this point along the width axis the flat segments at curve extremes are short and of little visible consequence, leaving a cleanly proportioned neo-grotesque suitable for all-purpose typesetting. Seven weights from Thin to Black with matching italics handle hierarchy from text to display, and the cut connects to all other widths and to a slant axis through the Lateral variable font.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Lateral as a contemporary superfamily revolving around sideways expansion. The Standard width is where the design principle of flat curve extremes is least visible, and the foundry frame the middle widths as intended for all-purpose typesetting.

name: Lateral Ultra Compressed
classification_notes: The narrowest width in the seven-width Lateral system; flat curve segments yield emphatically straight-sided rounds and brutalist verticals.
summary: The narrowest cut of Lateral: an ultra-compressed neo-grotesque with brutalist straight-sided rounds, in seven weights with italics, sized for display.
description: Lateral Ultra Compressed is the narrowest width in the seven-width Lateral system. The flat segments at curve extremes are at their most pronounced, producing emphatically straight-sided rounds and brutalist verticals; the foundry note that the family's widest width takes more than five times the space of this narrowest one. Seven weights from Thin to Black with matching italics suit posters, mastheads and other display work where vertical economy is the point.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Lateral as a contemporary superfamily revolving around sideways expansion, with the widest width taking up more than five times as much space as the narrowest. They frame the extreme widths as lending themselves to display use.

name: Lateral Ultra Wide
classification_notes: The widest width in the seven-width Lateral system; flat curve segments stretch into long horizontal parts.
summary: The widest cut of Lateral: an ultra-wide neo-grotesque with long horizontal parts, in seven weights with italics, sized for display.
description: Lateral Ultra Wide is the widest width in the seven-width Lateral system. The flat segments at curve extremes stretch into long horizontal bars, giving the cut its slow, brutalist stance; the foundry note that this width takes up more than five times the space of Lateral Ultra Compressed. Seven weights from Thin to Black with matching italics suit headlines, mastheads and statement display work, with continuous interpolation to all other widths through the Lateral variable font.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Lateral as Schick Toikka's first variable font family. They highlight that the widest width takes up more than five times as much space as the narrowest, and frame the extreme widths as suited to display use.

name: Lyyra
classification_notes: Schick Toikka cite Edward Johnston's Railway Type (1918) and Roger Excoffon's Antique Olive (1960s) as the few predecessors for Lyyra's bare, vertically cut stroke endings; the high-waisted caps channel German grotesks from the 1910s.
summary: An expressive sans across Standard, Extended and Expanded widths in five weights with italics; counters bloom into petals and stroke endings cut vertically.
description: Lyyra is a typeface with a radical aesthetic that alternates between the organic and the mechanical. Its lowercase counters start from a point and grow into acute symmetrical petals; arches start low and ascend slowly, like tree branches, and stroke endings are all cut vertically for a bare look. The high-waisted caps channel German grotesks from the 1910s. The family eschews condensed widths in favour of more luxuriant Extended and downright decadent Expanded styles, with each of the three widths spanning five weights with matching italics. Lining figures sit alongside oldstyle, tabular and sub-/superscript numerals plus fractions; rounded alternates are available where the default diamond dots are too daring.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Lyyra as a typeface with a radical aesthetic that alternates between the organic and the mechanical. They cite Johnston's Railway Type (1918) and Excoffon's Antique Olive (1960s) as rare predecessors for the vertically cut endings, and frame the family as one that eschews condensed widths for more luxuriant Extended and downright decadent Expanded styles.

name: Lyyra Expanded
classification_notes: The widest cut in the Lyyra system; the foundry describe it as 'downright decadent', taking the family's diagonality to extremes.
summary: The expanded cut of Lyyra: a decadent five-weight expressive sans with italics, taking the family's diagonality to extremes for display use.
description: Lyyra Expanded is the widest cut in the Lyyra system. The foundry describe it as 'downright decadent', taking the family's diagonal counters and bloomed petals to extremes for powerful display work. Five weights from Light to Black with matching italics keep the cut workable across hierarchy, and it pairs naturally with Lyyra Standard and Lyyra Extended. Default diamond dots can be swapped for tamer round alternates, and the OpenType feature set covers oldstyle, tabular and scientific figures alongside fractions.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka frame Lyyra as eschewing condensed widths for more luxuriant Extended and downright decadent Expanded styles. The Expanded cut is where the family's diagonality is carried to extremes, with the foundry positioning it as a powerful display option free of patina.

name: Lyyra Extended
classification_notes: The middle wider cut in the Lyyra system; the foundry describe the wider Lyyra styles as 'luxuriant' and free of patina.
summary: The extended cut of Lyyra: a luxuriant five-weight expressive sans with italics, sitting between Standard and the more decadent Expanded.
description: Lyyra Extended sits one notch wider than Lyyra Standard and one notch narrower than Lyyra Expanded. The foundry describe the wider Lyyra styles as 'luxuriant' and free of patina, making the Extended cut a powerful display option that retains the family's high-waisted caps, petalled counters and vertically cut endings. Five weights from Light to Black with matching italics, plus full OpenType figures and rounded dot alternates, give it the range needed for editorial and identity work.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe the wider Lyyra cuts as eschewing condensed widths for more luxuriant Extended and downright decadent Expanded styles. The Extended sits between the Standard and Expanded widths, where the family's diagonality is increasingly amplified.

name: Lyyra Standard
classification_notes: The default width of Lyyra; in capitals it can acquire monumental qualities, in mixed case it reads as a self-confident face suitable for editorial design and beyond.
summary: The standard width of Lyyra: an expressive sans in five weights with italics, with petalled counters and vertically cut endings.
description: Lyyra Standard is the default width of the Lyyra system. The foundry frame it as a self-confident face for mixed-case editorial work that can acquire monumental qualities when set in all caps. Five weights from Light to Black with matching italics carry the family's signature petalled counters, high-waisted caps and vertically cut stroke endings. The OpenType feature set covers lining, oldstyle, tabular and sub-/superscript figures plus fractions, and round dot alternates replace the default diamond dots where needed.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Lyyra as a typeface with a radical aesthetic that alternates between the organic and the mechanical. They cite Johnston's Railway Type (1918) and Excoffon's Antique Olive (1960s) as the few predecessors for the vertically cut endings, and the Standard width is where mixed-case editorial work feels most settled.

name: Noe
classification_notes: Schick Toikka link Noe to the 'Latin' genre (also known in German as 'Etienne' or 'Renaissance'), which first blossomed in the 1880s. Noe shares the older rational romans' vertical stress axis and strong contrast but is distinguished by tapered triangular serifs.
summary: A high-contrast Latin serif with wedge-shaped serifs and beaked arches, in three optical sizes (Display, Standard, Text) with italics and broad European language support.
description: Noe is a high-contrast serif in the Latin (Etienne / Renaissance) genre that first blossomed in the 1880s. Large wedge-shaped serifs, beaked arches and a vertical stress axis with strong thick-thin contrast give it a fierce edge, tempered by a graceful italic with a blatantly cursive construction and long tapering entry and exit strokes. Three optical subfamilies tune the design to size: Noe Display is unapologetically expressive, with delicate hairlines for headlines; Noe Text adds heft and blunts corners just enough to keep details visible at body sizes; Noe Standard sits between them for subheads, intros and big screen sizes. Each comes in a range of weights plus italics and ships with a Latin Extended character set covering virtually every language of Western and Central Europe.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe what makes Noe unique as 'the audacious way its strokes end', citing the historical Latin or Etienne genre as its lineage. They frame the three optical subfamilies as a system: Display for expression, Text for body copy, Standard for the middle ground including subheads, intros, pull quotes and big sizes on screen.

name: Noe Display
classification_notes: The display optical size of Noe; the foundry describe it as 'unapologetically expressive', with sparkle and bite suited to large sizes.
summary: Noe's display optical size: an expressive seven-weight Latin serif for headlines, with delicate hairlines and italics.
description: Noe Display is the largest-size cut of the Noe family. The foundry describe it as unapologetically expressive, with sparkle and bite designed for large sizes where letters can wave their own peculiar flag. Wedge-shaped serifs, beaked arches and a vertical stress axis give it a fierce, fashion-magazine edge, while the italic stays graceful with a blatantly cursive construction and long tapering exits. Seven weights from Thin to Black with matching italics make it usable across detailed display hierarchies, paired down through Noe Standard to Noe Text for body copy.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Noe Display as speaking with clarity and confidence, but tempered by a graceful discipline. They flag it as distinguished display type with sparkle and bite, the most expressive cut in the family's three-tier optical system.

name: Noe Standard
classification_notes: The middle optical size of Noe; the foundry describe it as suited to subheads, intros, pull quotes and big sizes on screen, where Display would be too delicate and Text too rugged.
summary: Noe's middle optical size: a seven-weight Latin serif tuned for subheads, intros, pull quotes and large sizes on screen.
description: Noe Standard is the middle optical size of the Noe family. The foundry describe it as the right cut for situations where Noe Display would be too delicate and Noe Text too rugged, including subheads, intros, pull quotes and large sizes on screen. Seven weights from Thin to Black with matching italics carry the family's wedge serifs, beaked arches and high contrast at this scale, paired up to Noe Display for headlines and down to Noe Text for body copy.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Noe Standard as sitting between Display and Text, perfect for subheads, intros, pull quotes, as well as for big sizes on screen. They frame Noe's three subfamilies as naturally pairing well with each other and playing well on their own.

name: Noe Text
classification_notes: The text optical size of Noe; the foundry describe it as adding heft to hairlines and blunting corners just enough to keep details visible at reduced sizes, with broader proportions and more open counters for smaller type and longer passages of copy. Includes a Book weight that the Display and Standard styles do not.
summary: Noe's text optical size: a sturdier eight-weight Latin serif (including Book) tuned for body copy and long-form reading.
description: Noe Text is the smallest-size cut of the Noe family. The foundry describe it as a text companion that functions in body copy: heft is added to hairlines, corners are blunted just enough to keep details visible at reduced sizes, and proportions broaden with more open counters for smaller type and longer passages. Eight weights from Thin to Black including a Book weight, all with matching italics, give it the range to handle long-form editorial work, paired up through Noe Standard to Noe Display in the family's three-tier optical system.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Noe Text as developed as a text companion that functions in body copy. It adds heft to the hairlines, and blunts the corners just enough to keep the details visible at reduced sizes while maintaining the crisp character of the family overall.

name: Phonic
classification_notes: Schick Toikka frame Phonic's softened acute junctions as a stylistic feature evoking machine-made lettering, specifically signs made with routed or engraved letters where junctions are limited by how the milling head can move.
summary: A precise sans with rounded acute junctions evoking routed signage, paired with a monospaced companion; four weights from Light to Bold with italics.
description: Phonic is a sans-serif family characterised by technical precision with sensory detail. Wherever two strokes meet at an angle smaller than 90 degrees the junction is rounded, evoking machine-made lettering and signs cut with a milling head. Other corners are as sharp as expected, producing a compelling mix of straight and round. Inner forms in glyphs like b and g are smooth and undisturbed by the verticals; italics slope only as much as needed to set themselves apart in text; and round dots reinforce a quietly affable quality. Phonic comes alongside a monospaced sibling, Phonic Mono, that embraces fixed character widths and amplifies the technical timbre. Both subfamilies were drawn in four weights from Light to Bold, with stylistic alternates that swap round dots and punctuation for square ones.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Phonic as built for precision but with a human touch: most strokes are terminated perpendicularly but some end diagonally for sensory effect, and the round dots play to its affable quality. They flag the proportions as compact rather than condensed, with rounds slightly oblong, and the family is drawn in four weights from Light to Bold across roman, italic and mono subfamilies.

name: Phonic Mono
classification_notes: The monospaced sibling of Phonic; the foundry describe it as embracing the limitations of fixed character widths and emphasising the allure of the technical, with the same softened acute junctions found in the proportional Phonic.
summary: The monospace companion to Phonic: four weights from Light to Bold with italics, fixed widths, rounded acute junctions and square-dot stylistic alternates.
description: Phonic Mono is the monospaced subfamily of Phonic. It carries over the parent's softened acute junctions, perpendicular and occasional diagonal terminals and round dots, and recasts them inside fixed character widths that emphasise the allure of the technical. Four weights from Light to Bold, all with italics, suit code, technical documentation and any setting where Phonic's machine-made cadence becomes the point. A stylistic alternate set swaps round dots and punctuation for square ones.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Phonic Mono as entering a game with different rules, embracing the limitations of fixed character widths and emphasising the allure of the technical. Both Phonic subfamilies were drawn in four weights from Light to Bold.

name: Saol
classification_notes: Schick Toikka cite three American Victorian serifs as Saol's reference points: Ronaldson (MacKellar, Smiths & Jordan, 1884), Caxton Old Style (Marder, Luse & Co, 1889) and West Old Style (Barnhart Brothers & Spindler, 1892), each with old-style structure but exaggerated terminals, thin hairlines and truncated descenders.
summary: A Victorian-old-style serif system across Display, Standard and Text optical sizes, with flamboyant swash italic and an additional Mono cut.
description: Saol interprets late-1800s American Victorian serifs (Ronaldson 1884, Caxton Old Style 1889, West Old Style 1892) for present-day use, keeping the eccentric spirit of its sources. Diagonal strokes cross conventional boundaries (A, M, W, k, y), shoulders are unusually taut (h, n, u) and razor-edge details abound (E, G, R, T, t). The italic is particularly flamboyant, looping and undulating at a fairly deep slant, narrower than the roman but comparable in colour for harmonious mixing within a line. Borrowing wisdom from metal type's size-specific reality, Saol comes in three optical sizes: Display has delicate hairlines and maximum contrast, Standard is intended for medium use, and Text is optimised for smaller sizes with modest contrast. A Monospaced style adds another option for curious typography.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Saol as designed for creating detailed typographic hierarchies, from striking headlines to pleasantly readable body copy. They flag it as suited to magazines and other publications seeking a lavish flair, or a voice that is confidently enigmatic, and note that the Monospaced style invites readers to ponder the origin of this time-traveling type.

name: Saol Display
classification_notes: The display optical size of Saol; the foundry describe it as featuring delicate hairlines for maximum contrast.
summary: Saol's display optical size: a high-contrast Victorian old-style serif for headlines, with delicate hairlines and flamboyant swash italic.
description: Saol Display is the largest-size cut of Saol. The foundry describe it as featuring delicate hairlines for maximum contrast, drawn for striking headlines and titling. Six weights from Thin to Bold with matching italics include opulent swash italic capitals, and connect down through Saol Standard for middle sizes and Saol Text for body copy. The Victorian sources behind the family (Ronaldson 1884, Caxton Old Style 1889, West Old Style 1892) come through most fiercely here, with razor-edge details and exaggerated terminals.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Saol Display as part of a comprehensive palette designed for creating detailed typographic hierarchies. While Saol Display features delicate hairlines for maximum contrast, Saol Standard is intended for medium size use and Saol Text is optimised for smaller sizes with modest contrast.

name: Saol Standard
classification_notes: The middle optical size of Saol; the foundry describe it as intended for medium-size use, between the delicate Display and the sturdier Text.
summary: Saol's middle optical size: a Victorian old-style serif for medium sizes, between the delicate Display and sturdier Text.
description: Saol Standard is the medium-size cut of the Saol family. The foundry describe it as intended for medium size use, sitting between Saol Display's delicate hairlines and Saol Text's modest contrast and sturdier build. Six weights from Thin to Bold with matching italics carry the family's exaggerated Victorian terminals, taut shoulders and flamboyant swash italic at this scale, useful for subheads, intros, pull quotes and decks where the Display would be too delicate.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Saol Standard as intended for medium size use within the family's three-tier optical system, paired with Saol Display for maximum contrast headlines and Saol Text for smaller sizes with modest contrast.

name: Saol Text
classification_notes: The text optical size of Saol; the foundry describe it as optimised for smaller sizes with modest contrast. Includes a Book weight that the Display and Standard styles do not.
summary: Saol's text optical size: a Victorian old-style serif for body copy with modest contrast, a Book weight, and flamboyant swash italic.
description: Saol Text is the smallest-size cut of the Saol family. The foundry describe it as optimised for smaller sizes with modest contrast, with the family's Victorian terminals and taut shoulders softened just enough to remain readable in body copy. Seven weights from Thin to Bold including a Book weight, all with matching italics, give it editorial range, and the flamboyant swash italic carries through. Pair it up to Saol Standard and Saol Display for headlines and subheads.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Saol Text as optimised for smaller sizes with modest contrast, the body-copy partner in a comprehensive palette designed for creating detailed typographic hierarchies, from striking headlines to pleasantly readable body copy.

name: Scto Grotesk
classification_notes: Schick Toikka frame Scto Grotesk as their take on the neo-grotesque genre that became an essential ingredient of the International Typographic Style. They opt for the least quirky, most rational option throughout: strokes are invariably cut horizontally or vertically across all weights, R has a straight leg, dots are square, ascenders and caps share the same height.
summary: A two-flavour neo-grotesque (A and B) in six weights with italics; B is narrower than A but not a condensed, for economical set widths.
description: Scto Grotesk is Schick Toikka's neo-grotesque, drawn with the explicit goal of being the most rational, least quirky version of the genre. Strokes are invariably cut horizontally or vertically across all weights; R has a straight leg, dots are square, ascenders and caps share the same height. Reader-friendly details such as the lowered dots on i and the asymmetric bars in f and t keep it animated rather than sterile. The family comes in two flavours: Scto Grotesk A is beaming with confidence; Scto Grotesk B is narrower but not a condensed, intended for economical set widths. Italics slope only as much as needed for inline emphasis, making them usable for longer texts. The naming pays tribute to typefaces that didn't yet have names of their own and were identified by foundry or designer plus a style descriptor, and the family ships with an extended Latin character set and circled-number extras.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Scto Grotesk as their very own take on the late-1800s grotesque, redrawn in the mid-20th century into a systematic neo-grotesque. They flag the family as decidedly matter-of-fact but not sterile, with two flavours (A and B) where B serves well when a more economical set width is in order, and they note the naming as a tribute to typefaces identified by foundry or designer rather than by a unique name.

name: Scto Grotesk A
classification_notes: The wider flavour of Scto Grotesk; the foundry describe it as 'beaming with confidence'.
summary: Scto Grotesk's wider flavour: a confident neo-grotesque in six weights with italics, drawn for the rational, least-quirky version of the genre.
description: Scto Grotesk A is the wider of the family's two flavours. The foundry describe it as 'beaming with confidence', with strokes cut horizontally or vertically across all weights, a straight-legged R, square dots and matched ascenders and cap heights. Six weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, italics that slope only as much as needed for inline emphasis, an extended Latin character set and useful extras such as circled numbers make it a versatile workhorse for identity, editorial and UI work. Pair with Scto Grotesk B for a more economical set width.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Scto Grotesk A as beaming with confidence, the all-purpose flavour in a family that strives for the ultimate grotesque by always opting for the least quirky, most rational option.

name: Scto Grotesk B
classification_notes: The narrower flavour of Scto Grotesk; the foundry stress it is narrower than A but not a condensed, intended for economical set widths.
summary: Scto Grotesk's narrower flavour: a neo-grotesque tuned for economical set widths in six weights with italics, but not a condensed.
description: Scto Grotesk B is the narrower of the family's two flavours. The foundry stress that B is narrower than A but is not a condensed; instead it serves well when a more economical set width is in order, while keeping all the family's rational details: horizontal and vertical stroke ends across all weights, a straight-legged R, square dots, matching ascenders and caps. Six weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, an extended Latin character set and circled-number extras make it a workhorse partner to Scto Grotesk A.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Scto Grotesk B as narrower than A but not a condensed; it serves well when a more economical set width is in order. Italics slope just as much as necessary to work for inline emphasis, rendering them suitable also for longer texts.

name: Spot
subcategory: typewriter-derived sans / proportional and monospace system
classification_notes: Schick Toikka note that Spot's monospace came first and informs the whole family: the uniform stroke width, rounded ends and distinctive dots evoke a typebar hitting an inked ribbon. The wide proportions and large x-height also originate from this typewriter logic.
summary: A three-flavour family (proportional Grotesk, fixed-pitch Mono, hybrid Semi-Mono) in four weights with italics; a variable font ties them via a spacing axis.
description: Spot is an approachable sans serif family with a warm personality and a soft touch, available as proportional Spot Grotesk, fixed-pitch Spot Mono and hybrid Spot Semi-Mono, optionally as a single variable font. The Mono came first and shapes the whole family: a uniform stroke width, rounded ends and distinctive dots evoke a typebar striking an inked ribbon, while the wide proportions and large x-height read as restrained capitals that produce even texture in body copy. Wide letters and open spacing keep it readable in marginalia and captions, while larger sizes bring its overall roundness to the fore as a friendly display option. The variable version drives weight, slant and a spacing axis that tunes the exact degree of monospacedness, with intermediate instances producing tiny stubs on narrow glyphs that grow into wide bars in the Mono. Each of the three branches has four weights upright and italic, with Latin Extended, sub- and superscripts, innovative fractions and a kit of playful and functional icons.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Spot as comfortably setting body copy just as well as it delivers plump headlines, with a quiet dignity and subtle smile. They flag that the Mono came first, which is unusual; the proportional Grotesk and the hybrid Semi Mono share Spot Mono's appeal of provisionality, austerity and authenticity without the drawbacks of fixed character widths.

name: Spot Grotesk
classification_notes: The proportionally spaced flavour of Spot, sharing the family's typewriter origins (uniform stroke width, rounded ends, distinctive dots, wide proportions, large x-height) without fixed character widths.
summary: Spot's proportional flavour: a typewriter-derived sans in four weights with italics, with the Mono's warmth but without fixed character widths.
description: Spot Grotesk is the proportionally spaced flavour of the Spot family. It carries Spot Mono's appeal of provisionality, austerity and authenticity, including the uniform stroke width, rounded ends, distinctive dots and large x-height, but trades fixed character widths for proportional rhythm. Four weights from Light to Bold with matching italics suit body copy, captions and marginalia in small sizes and friendly display work at larger sizes. It pairs with Spot Mono and Spot Semi-Mono, and a Spot variable font ties all three with weight, slant and spacing axes.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Spot Grotesk as offering the same appeal of provisionality, austerity, and authenticity as Spot Mono, without the drawbacks of fixed character widths.

name: Spot Mono
classification_notes: The fixed-pitch flavour of Spot and the original starting point of the family; the foundry note that the Mono came first and shaped the whole family's appearance.
summary: Spot's fixed-pitch flavour and original starting point: a typewriter monospace in four weights with italics, with rounded ends and distinctive impressed dots.
description: Spot Mono is the fixed-pitch flavour of Spot and the family's starting point: the foundry note that the Mono came first and informs the whole family. Uniform stroke width, rounded ends and distinctive dots evoke a typebar hitting an inked ribbon and digging into the paper, while a wide stance and large x-height keep it readable. Four weights from Light to Bold with matching italics, plus innovative fractions, sub- and superscripts and a kit of playful and functional icons, suit code, technical documentation and warm display work alike. Pair with Spot Grotesk and Spot Semi-Mono.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Spot's special genesis as the Mono coming first, with the typewriter origin showing in details like the distinctive dots that evoke a typebar hitting an inked ribbon with force, digging into the paper. Spot Mono is the fixed-pitch member of the three-flavour family.

name: Spot Semi-Mono
classification_notes: The hybrid between Spot Grotesk and Spot Mono; the foundry describe instances between the Grotesk and the Semi Mono in the variable font as producing tiny stubs on narrow glyphs that grow into wide bars in the Mono.
summary: Spot's hybrid flavour: a semi-monospaced typewriter-derived sans in four weights with italics, sitting between the proportional Grotesk and fixed-pitch Mono.
description: Spot Semi-Mono is the hybrid flavour of Spot, sitting between the proportional Spot Grotesk and the fixed-pitch Spot Mono. It keeps the family's uniform stroke width, rounded ends, distinctive dots and large x-height while widening narrow glyphs partway towards Mono proportions, useful for tabular layouts that still need rhythm. Four weights from Light to Bold with matching italics, Latin Extended language coverage, sub- and superscripts, fractions and a kit of icons round it out, and a Spot variable font ties Grotesk, Semi-Mono and Mono on a continuous spacing axis.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka describe Spot's variable version as featuring three axes: weight, slant, and a third one to alter the spacing and tune the exact desired degree of 'monospacedness'. Especially with instances between the Grotesk and the Semi Mono, this option can lead to delightful and unexpected results, including tiny stubs on those narrow glyphs that grow wide bars in the Mono.

name: Trio Grotesk
subcategory: early grotesque revival (Kaart Antieke / Lettergieterij Amsterdam)
classification_notes: Schick Toikka describe Trio Grotesk as a revival of Kaart Antieke, published by Lettergieterij Amsterdam in 1909, the small-size society-printing grotesk that Piet Zwart used for his unpublished essay 'Van oude tot nieuwe typografie'. Florian Schick discovered the only two remaining copies at the Meermanno Museum in Den Haag.
summary: A three-weight revival of Kaart Antieke (Lettergieterij Amsterdam, 1909) with italics, preserving letterpress ink-spread features and a wide, generously spaced design.
description: Trio Grotesk is a revival of Kaart Antieke, a small-size grotesque published by Lettergieterij Amsterdam in 1909 for society printing: copperplate engraved invitations, announcements, menus, stationery and calling cards. The face was used by Piet Zwart for his never officially published essay 'Van oude tot nieuwe typografie', and Florian Schick discovered the only two remaining copies at the Meermanno Museum in Den Haag. Rather than working from the metal type itself, Schick Toikka enlarged and examined the printed image to replicate the impression as faithfully as possible, preserving features unique to letterpress such as soft stroke endings and junctions caused by ink spread and pressure, alongside the wide stance, loose spacing and large lowercase required of such diminutive type. The single found style is expanded to three weights plus italics with Latin Extended character sets, OpenType figure sets and a batch of arrows, ornaments and dingbats.
foundry_blurb: Schick Toikka recount that on a visit to the Meermanno Museum in Den Haag, Florian Schick discovered the only two remaining copies of Zwart's essay and, struck by the historical value of the booklet, the foundry decided to revive the typeface it was set in. They worked from the printed image rather than the metal to preserve letterpress ink-spread features.
